questions.question: Explain the Weighted Interval Scheduling Problem and how it differs from the unweighted version.
keyPoints: WISP selects non-overlapping intervals to maximize total weight
Each interval has an associated profit/weight value
Unweighted maximizes number of intervals, weighted maximizes sum of weights
WISP requires dynamic programming, unweighted can use greedy

questions.question: Define p(i) in the context of Weighted Interval Scheduling and explain how it is computed.
keyPoints: p(i) is the index of rightmost interval finishing before interval i starts
Computed using binary search on sorted finish times
If no such interval exists, p(i) = 0
Enables O(log n) lookup for each interval

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for the DP solution of Weighted Interval Scheduling and explain its components.
keyPoints: DP[i] = max(DP[i-1], weight[i] + DP[p(i)])
DP[i-1] represents excluding interval i
weight[i] + DP[p(i)] represents including interval i plus optimal solution for p(i)
Base case: DP[0] = 0

questions.question: Why does the greedy approach of selecting the earliest finishing interval fail for Weighted Interval Scheduling?
keyPoints: Greedy by earliest finish doesn't consider weights
A later interval may have much higher weight
Example: early finish low weight vs late finish high weight
Only optimal for unweighted case, not weighted

questions.question: Describe the steps to reconstruct the selected intervals in the Weighted Interval Scheduling DP solution.
keyPoints: Start from DP[n] and work backwards
If weight[i] + DP[p(i)] > DP[i-1], include interval i
Move to p(i) after including
Otherwise move to i-1
Continue until i = 0

questions.question: What are the time and space complexities of the Weighted Interval Scheduling DP algorithm? Explain.
keyPoints: Time: O(n log n) - sorting O(n log n), p(i) computation O(n log n), DP O(n)
Space: O(n) - storing intervals, finish times, and DP array
Can be optimized to O(1) space with modification

questions.question: Solve the following Weighted Interval Scheduling problem using dynamic programming. Intervals: I1(0,3,5), I2(2,5,6), I3(4,6,4), I4(5,7,2), I5(6,8,8). Format: Interval(start, end, weight). Show all steps including sorting, computing p(i), DP table, and final solution.
keyPoints: Selected intervals: I5 and I1 with total weight 13

questions.question: Write a complete algorithm for Weighted Interval Scheduling using dynamic programming. Include initialization, preprocessing of intervals, computation of p(i), the main DP loop, and reconstruction of the solution. Analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Sort intervals by finish time
Precompute finish times array
For each i, find p(i) using binary search
Initialize DP[0] = 0
For i from 1 to n: DP[i] = max(DP[i-1], weight[i] + DP[p(i)])
Backtrack to reconstruct solution
Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n)

questions.question: Prove that the Weighted Interval Scheduling problem exhibits optimal substructure property. How does this lead to the dynamic programming solution?
keyPoints: Optimal substructure: optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems
Consider optimal solution for first i intervals
Either includes interval i: weight[i] + optimal for p(i) intervals
Or excludes interval i: optimal for first i-1 intervals
This gives the recurrence DP[i] = max(DP[i-1], weight[i] + DP[p(i)])
Overlapping subproblems also exist, making DP efficient

questions.question: Discuss at least three real-world applications of Weighted Interval Scheduling. For each application, describe how the problem maps to intervals with weights.
keyPoints: Meeting scheduling: intervals are meeting times, weights are revenue/importance
Job sequencing: intervals are processing times with deadlines, weights are profits
Resource allocation: intervals are resource usage periods, weights are values
Airline crew scheduling: intervals are flight assignments, weights are costs/salaries
Room booking: intervals are booking times, weights are payment amounts
